coredata.dc:title: Visual detection of Auramine O using dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent nanopaper sensor combined portable smartphone
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenhui
Jayan, Heera
Majeed, Usman
Zou, Xiaobo
Hu, Qin
Guo, Zhiming
coredata.dc:description: The misuse of additives is a major problem for global food safety, and of particular concern is Auramine O (AO). Although the analysis of AO by fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been extensively explored, the development of effective sensitivity enhancement and portable detection solutions based on CDs remains a significant challenge. Herein, we developed a dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent probe (RFP) integrated with fluorescent nanopaper and smartphone application, which has been used to achieve portable visualization and quantitative detection of AO in food and drug samples. The specific response analysis model (RFP) for AO detection was constructed using fluorescence resonance energy transfer and electrostatic attraction between B-CDs and O-CDs. Within a concentration range of 0.0–10.0 μM, achieving a detection limit as low as 12.3 nM. Furthermore, a laboratory device that incorporates portable fluorescent nanopaper into a smartphone was developed to record the color information response ratio (R/B) in combination with the ColorColl application, enabling visual quantitative measurement of AO. This is powerful construction of RFP, which avoids time-consuming synthesis and complex modification. It directly demonstrates the superiority of proportional fluorescence detection over single wavelength detection. This work provides a referable perspective for preliminary monitoring and rapid on-site detection of harmful factors such as AO in food and drug samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food safety
Carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visualization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Method accuracy validation by HPLC analysis
Fluorescence response of RFP to AO
The quenching mechanism of RFP towards AO
Application of RFP-incorporated nanopaper for AO sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)The TEM image of B-CDs. Inset: Particle size distribution of B-CDs. (B) The TEM image of O-CDs. Inset: Particle size distribution of O-CDs. (C) The FTIR spectrum of B-CDs. (D) the fluorescence emission spectra of RFP under excitations from 350 to 430 nm. Inset: Changes in I0/I of RFP at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of B-CDs (0.01−1.0 mg/mL). (B) The effect of different concentrations of O-CDs on RFP detection performance, and the inset shows the effect of different concentrations of O-CDs on I0/I. (C) The effect of different pH on I0/I. (D)Effects of different reaction times on I0/I.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of RFP solutions in the presence of different concentrations of AO (0.0–40.0 μM). The inset shows the fluorescence image of RFP solution mixed with different concentrations of AO (0.0–100.0 μM) under UV light. (B) The linear plot of I0/I against AO. (C) CIE chromatogram of RFP in the presence of different concentrations of AO (0.0–40.0 μM). (D) The effect of other potentially interfering substances (50.0 μM) on the I0/I of the RFP and the mixed system of RFP + AO.

(A) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-AO, fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of B-CDs. (B) The fluorescence decay curves of RFP before (black line) and after (red line) reaction with AO (50.0 μM). (C) The Zeta potential diagrams of RFP, AO, and RFP + AO. (D) The UV–vis absorption spectra of RFP spiked with different concentrations of AO (0.0–40.0 μM).

Visual monitoring of AO concentration (0.0–100.0 μM) using (A) smartphone application with (C) fluorescent nanopaper, photo images were taken under 365 nm UV light. (B) Linear plot between R/B and AO concentration for the smartphone-assisted sensor and (D) linear plot between R/B and AO content for the fluorescent nanopaper.
(A) Schematic diagram of RFP synthesis and detection; (B) Schematic diagram of dual-mode channel construction.
Comparison of the detection parameters of the proposed RFP method with other reported analytical methods.

entry.cross-refs: Wang, Yang, Xiao, Han, et al. (2023)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RFP-based fluorescence and HPLC compared AO detection results in food and drugs.
Results of RFP-based smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform and fluorescent nanopaper sensing platform combined with smartphone application to detect AO in food and drug samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Visual detection of Auramine O using dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent nanopaper sensor combined portable smartphone

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The misuse of additives is a major problem for global food safety, and of particular concern is Auramine O (AO). Although the analysis of AO by fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has been extensively explored, the development of effective sensitivity enhancement and portable detection solutions based on CDs remains a significant challenge. Herein, we developed a dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent probe (RFP) integrated with fluorescent nanopaper and smartphone application, which has been used to achieve portable visualization and quantitative detection of AO in food and drug samples. The specific response analysis model (RFP) for AO detection was constructed using fluorescence resonance energy transfer and electrostatic attraction between B-CDs and O-CDs. Within a concentration range of 0.0–10.0 μM, achieving a detection limit as low as 12.3 nM. Furthermore, a laboratory device that incorporates portable fluorescent nanopaper into a smartphone was developed to record the color information response ratio (R/B) in combination with the ColorColl application, enabling visual quantitative measurement of AO. This is powerful construction of RFP, which avoids time-consuming synthesis and complex modification. It directly demonstrates the superiority of proportional fluorescence detection over single wavelength detection. This work provides a referable perspective for preliminary monitoring and rapid on-site detection of harmful factors such as AO in food and drug samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The misuse of additives represents a significant global food safety concern, with Auramine O (AO) emerging as a particularly worrisome substance due to its potential cumulative negative effects on human health and the environment. Numerous researchers have confirmed carcinogenic, teratogenic, and DNA damage in liver, kidneys, and bone marrow (Huang et al., 2024; Wang. 2023; Yan et al., 2016), which has led to a strict ban on its use as an additive in food in numerous other countries, including the United States, Japan, Europe and China (Ghaedi et al., 2015; Thuong Nguyen Thi et al., 2019; Tung et al., 2017). However, AO has been used extensively for dyeing of textiles, leather and wood-dyed products (Lu et al., 2020; Shabaan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017). Due to their capacity to impart visually appealing colors (Zhang et al., 2020), AO have been illegally and widely used in recent years to enhance the visual appeal of foods and drugs (Abe et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2023), posing a significant risk to human health. Although AO has been identified as a harmful food additive, its use in the food and drug industry is still showing an upward trend. Therefore, the development of accurate and sensitive methods for the detection of illegal additives in food and drugs is of great importance and urgency.

In recent years, analytical techniques of AO have remained focused on laboratory instrumental detection, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Tatebe et al., 2014), liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Li et al., 2014), electrochemical analysis (Hoa et al., 2020), and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Zhang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2024a) etc. These methods are objective, accurate and reliable, but the detection equipment is bulky, expensive and time-consuming. Due to such limitations, the development of portable and accurate real-time analytical methods for the detection of AO in food and drugs is crucial. Recently, the fluorescence method has demonstrated several advantageous characteristics, including simple operation, rapid response, a low detection limit, minimal sample requirement, high selectivity, and low analytical cost (Li et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021). As a result, it has emerged as a promising alternative for on-site visual detection of hazardous factors in food (Xue et al., 2024; Yin et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024b). These approaches have proven effective in rapidly identifying hazardous factors in food (Chen et al., 2023; Cui et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2021). In particular, the rapid development of nano-fluorescent sensors based on carbon dots has shown a series of advantageous characteristics, including rapidity, sensitivity, selectivity and potential visualization.

Carbon dots (CDs) are defined as oxygenated, quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanoparticles having an average size of less than 10 nm (Zhang et al., 2021; Zhang & Lu, 2024). Owing to their low cost, optical tunability, robust photostability and biocompatibility (Jung et al., 2022; Yoo et al., 2019), CDs have attracted attention as fluorescent probes. Most of the previously documented methods for detecting AO involve the use of monochromatic fluorescent sensors. These sensors generally depend on alterations in the fluorescence intensity of single-emitting CDs' emission peaks at a specific excitation wavelength to yield the desired results (Zhu et al., 2021). Zhu et al. designed CDs to detection of AO with a detection limit of 54.0 nM (Zhu et al., 2015). In their study, Wang et al. employed blue-fluorescent CDs as sensing elements for ultra-sensitive detection of AO in food and drug samples, achieving detection limits as minimal as 36.3 nM (Wang, Yang, Xiao, Guan, et al., 2023). However, conventional CDs inevitably exhibit certain shortcomings, including low light stability due to monochromatic variations and poor visual discrimination, which limit the progress of their potential applications. Significantly, the development of ratiometric fluorescent probes utilizing the dual-signal output of CDs offers novel insights into the monitoring target analyte concentrations (Alhazzani et al., 2024; Song et al., 2017; Wang, Yang, Xiao, Guan, et al., 2023). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence probes contain two different emission wavelengths, which facilitates the distinction of color variations and significantly enhances detection accuracy through the analysis of emission intensity ratios, providing built-in correction for environmental effects (Li et al., 2020; Su et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2024). The ratiometric fluorescence strategy facilitates superior visual detection based on a comprehensive range of color variations and enhances detection accuracy (Chu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2017). Furthermore, the potential of ratiometric probes in conjunction with smartphones to create a portable visualization and sensing platform offers new perspectives for rapid on-site detection of targets.

In this research, a novel smartphone analytical platform consisting of a ratiometric fluorescence system was developed, and a color recognition application for smartphones (provided by ColorColl) was presented as a means for enabling sensitive, real-time, and visual detection of AO in food and drugs (Scheme 1 ). As the core of the sensing system, blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) were coupled with orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) through fluorescence resonance energy transfer and electrostatic attraction. The RFP obtained at a single excitation wavelength (380 nm) exhibited two distinct emission peaks at 436 nm and 592 nm, thus realizing the dual response of the RFP, and visual fluorescence sensing without the necessity for expensive instruments. As the concentration of AO increased, the orange fluorescence observed in the RFP remained unaltered, while the blue fluorescence was extinguished. This resulted in a notable change in the solution's color, from pink to orange. The portable fluorescent nanopaper was successfully prepared by immobilizing RFP probes on ordinary filter paper. Combined with the smartphone color recognition application, the corresponding color information (RGB values) was identified to construct a fluorescence sensing platform for visualization and rapid detection. Consequently, this ratiometric sensing strategy has broad potential for sensitive, rapid and visual quantitative detection of AO, thereby establishing the foundation for the construction of a reliable and convenient real-time detection platform for hazardous substances in food and drugs.

The rhodamine B dye was acquired from Beijing chemicals (Beijing, China). The Auramine O was purchased from Biotechnology and Biological Engineering Inc. (Shanghai, China). The ethanol absolute, acetonitrile, ammonium acetate, Na3PO4, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, and all inorganic salts were obtained from were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The o-phenylenediamine, citric acid and all amino acids were acquired from Aladdin Biotechnology Inc (Shanghai, China). The experiments samples were obtained from the local market (Yangzhou, Jiangsu). The ultrapure water used throughout the experiment was produced using Millipore-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA). All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and required no additional purification prior to use.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of B-CDs and O-CDs were obtained using Tecnai 12 TEM (Philips, Eindhoven, Netherlands) at accelerating voltage of 120 kV. The composition of elements within B-CDs and O-CDs was examined utilizing the Vario EL cube analyzer (Elementar, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy were recorded with t a Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectra were analyzed using F-98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang Technology Company, China). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra analysis was performed with an adoption UV-2550 absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs, O-CDs and RFP were recorded on the FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, England). The zeta potential was characterized using a BeNano 90 Zeta-potential analyzer (Bettersize, China). All fluorescence images were taken by a smartphone rear camera (iPhone 12, Apple, USA) under a 365 nm portable UV lamp (Longpro, China).

The RFP was prepared according to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and electrostatic self-assembly strategies between B-CDs and O-CDs, to achieving efficient assembly. Specifically, the B-CDs and O-CDs were synthesized with reference to the existing literature (Hu et al., 2021; Wang, Yang, Xiao, Guan, et al., 2023). The RFP sensing probes were composed of two distinct types of CDs assemblies. Overall, 600.0 μL of B-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) was combined with 60.0 μL of O-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) in a cuvette. Subsequently, the mixture was diluted to a total volume of 2.0 mL with ultrapure water to ensure uniform dispersion of the two CDs. To guarantee comprehensive completion of the reaction, the mixture was permitted to incubate at ambient temperature for 5 min. The RFP solution was successfully prepared in accordance with the aforementioned steps.

To achieve fluorescence detection of AO, B-CDs and O-CDs were introduced to be assembled as composite nanoprobes. Firstly, 600.0 μL of B-CDs (1.0 mg/mL), 60.0 μL of O-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) and 1340.0 μL of ultrapure water were taken to prepare the proportional fluorescence nanosensing system. To achieve optimal sensing performance in the fluorescence-based sensing system, the impact of forming RFP with varying concentrations of B-CDs and O-CDs on the detection of AO efficiency was investigated. The spectral characteristics of RFP were measured by fluorescence spectrometer. When excited at 380 nm, RFP exhibited two independent emission peaks at 436 nm and 592 nm. Different concentrations of AO were added to the RFP to record the fluorescence intensity of the sensor system at the emission peak of 436 nm and 592 nm, respectively. The efficiency of fluorescence quenching was calculated according to the I0/I rule, where I0 represents for F0-436/F0-592, and I is interpreted as F436/F592. The linear equation was obtained by establishing the relationship between I0/I and AO concentration, and the LOD was determined by the 3σ/Ksv. To ensure the reliability of the results, all fluorescence measurements were repeated five times and subjected to further statistical analysis.

To assess the selectivity and anti-interference ability of RFP for AO detection, it was tested against various substances, including cations and anions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Na+, Zn2+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−), other dyes (Sudan I, Metanil Yellow, New Coccine, Indigo, Amaranth), small molecules (glucose, sucrose) and amino acids (Leucine, L-Methionine, Glutamic acid, Glycine, sucrose). To verify the selectivity of RFP for AO recognition, 10.0 μL of AO (10.0 mM) and the aforementioned interfering substance (10.0 mM) were added to 2.0 mL of RFP solution, respectively (at this time, the concentrations of AO and interfering substance were fixed at 50.0 μM in 2.0 mL of RFP solution). The fluorescence intensity of the emission peaks at 436 nm and 592 nm of the RFP sensing system was recorded before and after the addition of the interfering substance at λ ex = 380 nm.

This study aimed to investigate the anti-interference performance of RFP and AO when they were co-exposed to a range of interfering substances. A solution of 10.0 μL of the aforementioned interfering substances (10.0 mM) was introduced into the RFP/AO mixed system, with the concentrations of both AO and the interfering ions fixed at 50.0 μM. The I0/I values were calculated at λ ex of 380 nm, and the fluorescence response of the RFP/AO sensing system to the potential interferences was evaluated.

The initial step involved the preparation of an RFP solution containing AO at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 100.0 μM. Color fluorescence images of the RFP/AO mixture were captured in a dark environment under 365 nm UV light via the rear camera of a smartphone. To ensure the same data acquisition conditions, the smartphone was positioned at a fixed angle and location to reduce background interference to the minimum possible level. The smartphone's color recognition application (ColorColl) was utilized to analyze the color data (RGB values) extracted from the captured images. At least three repetitions were conducted for each sample to allow for the inclusion of error bars.

Qualitative filter paper was chosen as the carrier for the nanosensor because of its good liquid absorption capacity (Hu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2023). Afterwards, non-fluorescent qualitative filter papers were cut into circles with a diameter of 2.0 cm. Subsequently, each sheet was soaked in RFP solution for a period of 30 min, after which the RFP-containing filter paper was allowed to dry naturally in air, thus functioning as fluorescent nanopaper for sensing purposes. The color data (RGB values) of the fluorescent nanopaper images was subsequently analyzed through the utilization of a smartphone application (ColorColl).

The actual samples used for the AO assay were in liquid form. Firstly, all samples were thoroughly ground into fine powder after drying and dissolved in a mixture of ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol (10 mL, v/v: 1:1). The supernatant was collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm; 15 min) and filtered through a polystyrene microporous filter membrane (pore size 0.22 μM) before use, and the extracts were used for subsequent testing experiments. To test the feasibility of RFP in the detection of real samples, 20.0 μL of each sample extract was combined with 2.0 mL of RFP. The fluorescence intensity of RFP at 436 nm and 592 nm (λ ex = 380 nm) was measured by fluorescence spectrometry, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency I0/I was calculated to compute the amount of AO in the actual samples. For the analysis of real samples, spiked recovery experiments were established for AO at three different concentration levels (0.1, 0.2, 0.3 μM), with each concentration being measured five times.

For the smartphone analysis platform, typically, the sample extract (20.0 μL) was fluorescently imaged with RFP solution (2.0 mL) under UV lamp (365 nm). During the visualization and detection of fluorescent nanopaper, firstly 2.0 mL of RFP solution was initially mixed with 20.0 μL of sample extract, respectively. Subsequently, the cut qualitative filter paper was submerged in the mixed solution for 1 h to ensure adequate adsorption. Afterwards, they were dried at 60 °C, respectively. The color characteristics of each nanopaper were analyzed and recorded under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation and R/B was calculated. To estimate the quantitative recoveries, three concentration levels of AO (5.0, 10.0, 20.0 μM) were added to each food sample extract.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method accuracy validation by HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Meanwhile, HPLC was introduced to detect the content of AO in real samples to verify the accuracy of the proposed method. Specifically, the assay was performed on a Shimadzu LC20A liquid chromatograph (Japan), the mobile phase was acetonitrile and 10.0 mM ammonium acetate (80:20, v/v), and the samples were separated on a C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm), the column temperature was controlled to be 35 °C, the flow rate was kept at 1.0 mL/min, and the injection volume was 20.0 μL with the UV wavelength of 431 nm, and the average was taken by repeating three times for each measurement. The results were averaged over three repetitions of each measurement.

The morphology and microstructure of B-CDs and O-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the B-CDs were uniformly distributed, had a regular shape, and were nearly spherical. The diameters of 100 particles were randomly analyzed from the TEM images and the particle size distribution was plotted using Nano Measurer v1.2 software, and it was found that the particle size distribution of B-CDs ranged from 1.66 to 3.40 nm, with an average diameter of 2.42 nm (Fig. 1A inset). The TEM images of the synthesized O-CDs revealed well-dispersed and uniform nanoparticles, which facilitated adequate mixing of the two CDs (Fig. 1B). The histogram of 100 randomly selected O-CDs particles indicates sizes ranging from 2.91 to 5.89 nm with an average size of 4.45 nm.

To investigate the chemical composition of the synthesized B-CDs and O-CDs, elemental analysis was performed, as summarized in Table S1, the B-CDs consisted of 8.36% N, 47.22% C, 32.63% H and 11.79% O, while the O-CDs consisted of 11.93% N, 47.26% C, 7.65% H and 33.16% O. To explore the surface groups of the composite scale fluorescent probes, we obtained FTIR spectra of B-CDs and O-CDs were obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 1C (black line), the absorption peaks of B-CDs at 3350 cm−1 and 3010–2710 cm−1 were belonged to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively. The C–H stretching vibration at 2680 cm−1, and the absorption peaks at 1900 cm−1 and 1600–1400 cm−1 were attributed to the C=O and C=C stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak corresponding to the intense stretching vibration absorption observed at 1580 cm−1 indicated the presence of COO–, effectively inhibiting the non-radiative recombination events on the surface of B-CDs and thereby augmenting the fluorescence quantum yield (Liu et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 1C (red line), the broad absorption bands of O-CDs in the regions of 3447 cm−1 and 2927–2358 cm−1 belong to the O–H and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively, and the absorption peaks at 1412–1338 cm−1 proves the presence of C–C, and the weak absorption peak at 1180 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring C–O. The presence of absorption bands associated with –COOH and N–H groups made O-CDs water-soluble, which facilitates their formation into RFP probes by electrostatic attraction with B-CDs probes in aqueous media.

The fluorescence characteristics of O-CDs, B-CDs and RFP were analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. S1A, the optimal excitation wavelength for B-CDs remains at 400 nm, with the highest fluorescence emission peak occurs at 436 nm in the fluorescence emission spectrum. The inset shows a picture of blue fluorescence emission from B-CDs under UV light. The fluorescence spectrum of O-CDs showed (Fig. S1B) that O-CDs has the strongest single emission feature at 592 nm at the excitation wavelength of 470 nm, and the illustration shows that O-CDs exhibit orange fluorescence under ultraviolet light. As illustrated in Fig. 1D, the excitation at 380 nm resulted in the emergence of two emission peaks at 436 nm and 592 nm for RFP. The inset of Fig. 1D, shows the quenching efficiency (I0/I) at different excitation wavelengths. The quenching efficiency reached the maximum at 380 nm, with a satisfactory fluorescence intensity at peaks 436 nm and 592 nm. Accordingly, for subsequent detection experiments, 380 nm was chosen as the most suitable excitation wavelength.

When the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, O-CDs and RFP were scanned separately under 380 nm excitation (Fig. S2A), B-CDs and O-CDs showed emission peaks at 436 nm and 592 nm, respectively. After mixing the two CDs, obvious fluorescence changes were observed. Firstly, the mixture showed pink fluorescence under UV lamp, the O-CDs effectively suppressed the fluorescence of the B-CDs, resulting in a significant quenching effect. This mixing process resulted in the formation of an RFP with dual emission characteristics, and the emission peaks were positioned at 436 nm and 592 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of RFP at 436 nm was markedly diminished by the addition of AO, whereas the emission intensity at 592 nm exhibited only a slight alteration. Consequently, the fluorescence quenching of O-CDs by AO is negligible. Therefore, B-CDs were used as a working probe in response to AO, and O-CDs were used as a reference signal. As shown in Fig. S2B, the spectral properties of the two CDs were explored, indicating that the emission spectra of the B-CDs partially overlapped with the absorption spectra of the O-CDs, which is the main condition for FRET generation.

As the concentration of O-CDs improved from 0.01 mg/mL to 0.05 mg/mL (Fig. S2C), a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed at 436 nm, while the emission peak at 592 nm gradually became obvious and remained relatively stable, and the RFP exhibited a significant color change under UV light. The fluorescence intensity ratio of RFP at 380 nm (F436/F592) showed a significant linear correlation with the concentration of O-CDs, and the correlation coefficient (R2) was as high as 0.984 (as shown in the illustration in Fig. S2C). This indicated that B-CDs and O-CDs can effectively function as working probes and reference probes respectively to construct an efficient and reliable ratio fluorescence sensing system. As seen in Fig. S2D, the zeta potential of B-CDs was −29.23 mV, while that of O-CDs was 5.72 mV, and when B-CDs were mixed with O-CDs, the zeta potential of RFP was reduced to −21.67 mV. The experimental results showed that the O-CDs induced a marked change in the charge state of B-CDs, indicating that the O-CDs could be spontaneously adsorbed onto the B-CDs by electrostatic attraction, and RFP is successfully constructed. This result could confirm the existence of the FRET model, in which B-CDs as electron donors are negatively charged due to abundant carboxyl groups and O-CDs as electron acceptors are positively charged due to abundant amino groups (Ma et al., 2022). This further confirmed the possibility that both CDs can be constructed into RFP by electrostatic attraction self-assembly and FRET mechanism.

The B-CDs is critical as a response signal for constructing RFP probes. As demonstrated by Fig. 2 A, the concentration of B-CDs varied from 0.01 to 1.0 mg/mL, and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum value when the concentration of B-CDs was 0.3 mg/mL. Therefore, 0.3 mg/mL was selected as the desirable concentration of B-CDs for RFP construction. The effect of O-CDs concentration on RFP detection performance is shown in Fig. 2B, where the emission peak of B-CDs at 436 nm continued to decrease as the concentration of O-CDs improved from 0.01 to 0.05 mg/mL, which can be explained by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between B-CDs and O-CDs. At an O-CDs concentration of 0.03 mg/mL, I0/I reached a maximum (inset of Fig. 2B). Therefore, the most effective concentration of O-CDs was determined to be 0.03 mg/mL. Subsequently, the effect of pH on the AO detection ability of the RFP system was investigated. The effect of pH on the signal response is shown in Fig. 2C, as the pH increased from acidic to alkaline, I0/I did not change significantly in the range of pH 3.0–10.0 and decreased significantly at pH 2.0 and pH 12.0, and I0/I reached a maximum when the RFP was dispersed in ultrapure water (pH 5.8). To simplify the experimental steps, RFP was dispersed in ultrapure water (pH 5.8) for subsequent experiments. The fluctuation of I0/I with pH is due to the dependence of fluorescence intensity on the charge states of RFP and RFP/AO, which is usually observed in CDs-based sensing platforms(Hu et al., 2021; Szapoczka et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2024). Since the B-CDs and O-CDs carry abundant hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine groups, the protonation and deprotonation of these hydrophilic functionalities at various pH values would definitely lead to changes in their charge states, which in turn result in changes in their luminescent properties(Hu et al., 2013, 2014, 2021). Finally, the effect of reaction time on the detection of AO by RFP was researched. Due to the directness of the spectral line characteristics displayed by the CDs, the effective handling of the dark background reduces the interference from the external environment and facilitates a faster response of the carbon point and the measured substance. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity ratio I0/I reached the maximum value within 1.0 min and remained stable for the following 40.0 min, demonstrating faster and more stable response to AO. This indicated that the reaction of RFP with AO can be completed within 1.0 min, thus 1.0 min is the optimal reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of RFP to AO
ce:section.ce:para: Meanwhile, the performance of monochromatic fluorescent sensors for AO detection was investigated. As shown in Fig. S3A, the addition of AO resulted in a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs and a gradual darkening of the fluorescence colors. The intensity of fluorescence displayed by B-CDs exhibited a direct correlation with the level of AO concentration, and the fitting yielded a linear relationship with R2 = 0.995 (Fig. S3B) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 49.5 nM. Subsequently, the effect of different concentrations of AO on O-CDs was investigated, and the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs decreased slightly with an increase of AO concentration (Fig. S3C). The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs and the concentration of AO is shown in Fig. S3D. This directly demonstrated the advantage of the dual-signal ratio fluorescent probe over single-wavelength detection, suggesting that RFP is an effective method to improve sensitivity of AO detection by single CDs. In addition, CIE chromaticity diagrams was used to verify the visualization potential of RFP for detecting AO. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, when the AO concentration increased from 0.0 to 40 μM, the coordinates moved from pink to orange, indicating that the naked eye could capture a continuous color change in the hue of the RFP. Therefore, the RFP formed by adding O-CDs to B-CDs has greater advantages in AO vision and rapid detection.

AO detection in real samples often involves complex external environmental interferences. Therefore, selectivity and anti-interference are other key factors in evaluating sensor performance. To investigate the selectivity of RFP, the fluorescence intensity of RFP solutions containing AO and other potentially competing substances (50.0 μM) were obtained, respectively. The interfering substances included various amino acids, anions, small molecules, and dyes. As shown in Fig. 3D, only the addition of AO significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of RFP, whereas adding other interfering substances had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of RFP, indicating that RFP has a high selectivity.

Meanwhile, the sensing efficiencies (I0/I) of the mixed system of RFP and AO (50.0 μM) in the presence of the above possible interferences were separately obtained to investigate the anti-interference ability of RFP. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of the RFP/AO detection system remained largely unaffected by the above interfering substances, indicating that the RFP/AO detection system has a high interference resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism of RFP towards AO
ce:section.ce:para:
B-CDs as working probes, it is crucial to study in detail the connection between the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and the UV–vis spectra of AO. The spectral information of B-CDs and AO displayed in Fig. 4 A, which showed that the Ex and Em of B-CDs significantly overlap with the UV–vis spectra of AO, indicating that AO has the capability to absorb the energy associated with both the excitation and emission light within the RFP system, confirming the existence of FRET or IFE processes. In addition, the influence of AO in the fluorescence lifetime of RFP was assessed by obtaining fluorescence decay maps before and after the interaction of RFP with AO. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence lifetime of RFP was significantly shortened by the addition of AO. In order to accurately describe this decay process, a double exponential function model was used for precision fitting of the obtained fluorescence decay curves.
I(t) = AM1exp(−t/τ1) + AM2exp(−t/τ2)
Where τ refers to the time constants of the radiation decay channel and AM is the corresponding amplitude. The fitting results are detailed in Table S2. The average fluorescence lifetime of RFP was 9.21 ns, which was shortened to 7.64 ns after reacting with AO, and the apparent shortening of the fluorescence lifetime suggests a dynamic interaction between the response of RFP and AO.

To investigate the possibility of an electrostatic interaction between RFP and AO, zeta potential measurements and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy analyses were taken. As shown in Fig. 4C, the zeta potential of RFP turned out to be −24.67 mV and that of AO was −1.11 mV, and both RFP and AO were electronegative, thus ruling out electrostatic interactions. When RFP reacted with AO, its zeta potential changed significantly. This may be due to the repulsion between oppositely charged ions, which can cause the accumulation of antagonistic ions near the surface, shielding the surface charge and leading to a decrease in the zeta potential of RFP.

Finally, the UV–visible absorption spectra of RFP/AO mixture systems with different AO contents (0.0–40.0 μM) were obtained. As shown in Fig. 4D, the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak at 436 nm of the RFP/AO mixture gradually increased with the increase of AO addition concentration, in which no new absorption peak was observed and the absorption band was not shifted, indicating that the interaction between RFP and AO did not form a new chemical compound. The results ruled out the existence of electrostatic interactions. In summary, due to the multi-mechanism synergy between FRET, IFE, and dynamic quench, the proposed RFP probe exhibited different dual-emission peaks corresponding signals to AO, which showed a proportional response pattern and eliminated the external influence.

To visualize the detection of AO, a smartphone APP (ColorColl) was used to identify the fluorescence RGB values. As the concentration of AO increased from 0.0 μM to 100.0 μM, under UV light in dark conditions, the RFP solution showed a continuous change from red to orange (shown in Fig. 5 A), which can be used as a prerequisite for the visual detection of AO by ColorColl. The change in RGB values in the RFP system was mainly shown as R (red) and B (blue) channel values. Therefore, the ratio of R and B values was chosen as the parameter for semi-quantitative analysis of AO. As shown in Fig. 5B, the R/B ratio exhibited a good linear correlation with the AO concentration in the range of 0.0–100.0 μM, with R2 = 0.987 and a LOD of 2.06 μM. These results indicated that the smartphone-based portable RFP platform can achieve visual quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of RFP-incorporated nanopaper for AO sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve on-site real-time monitoring of AO, a portable disposable fluorescent nanopaper sensor was established to solve the dilemma of bulky instruments and complicated operation. The performance of paper sensors embedded with RFP was evaluated by extracting RGB information from RFP nanopaper photographs with different concentrations of AO using the Colorcoll application, with R/B as the fitting parameter. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescent nanopaper-based system produced identifiable color shifts very similar to the RFP solution-based system in the range of 0.0–100.0 μM. The correlation between the R/B ratio and the AO concentration is shown in Fig. 5D. The R/B value showed a good linear relationship with the AO concentration in the range of 0.0–100.0 μM (R2 = 0.983), and the LOD was measured to be 2.65 μM. The results showed that combining the smartphone with fluorescent nanopaper could achieve rapid quantitative analysis of AO in practical application scenarios.

In addition, the practicality of employing a smartphone app for visually identifying AO in the above sample extracts was investigated (Table 3 ). Photographs of the RFP/AO mixture and fluorescent nanopaper under UV light at 365 nm were obtained and chromatically analyzed using a smartphone application (ColorColl), respectively, and the R/B values were recorded. For quantitative recovery tests, three different concentration levels of AO were introduced into each sample filtrate, including 5.0, 10.0 and 20.0 μM. The spiked recoveries for actual samples were 94.0–121.4% and 95.4–113.1%, with RSD of 4.46 and 4.25, respectively. The newly constructed visualization method in this paper showed good agreement with the results obtained by HPLC method and fluorescence method (Table 2). The results showed that smartphone sensing platform analyses are accurate and have potential for new applications in various sensing applications.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescence probe (RFP) with dual emission signals was constructed and applied for highly sensitive selective determination and visual fluorescence detection of AO in real samples. This RFP probe combines the advantages of good selectivity and anti-interference of single-signal B-CDs, with the RFP built-in calibration capability of dual-signal output. This effectively overcomes the shortcomings of single-emission probes, which are susceptible to interference from environmental factors and improves the sensitivity of the sensing system. The RFP showed dual emission peaks at 436 nm and 592 nm under 380 nm excitation. With the introduction of AO, it resulted in different degrees of quenching for the double emission peaks of RFP. The detection limit of this method for the target AO was up to 12.3 nM. The sensing mechanism of fluorescence relied on variations in the quenching intensity of RFP emission peaks, specifically at wavelengths of 436 nm and 592 nm, achieved through processes such as IFE, FRET, and dynamic interaction. Additionally, the fluorescence color of the RFP changed significantly from pink to orange upon AO addition, which allowed the development of a portable fluorescent nanopaper. This sensing scheme achieved a new dual-mode proportional fluorescence detection with satisfactory specificity and sensitivity. In addition, the RFP-based fluorescent nanopaper has been successfully prepared and applied to the real sample, which verified its potential in field applications. In future research, we will build on the core strengths of the ratiometric fluorescent sensors constructed in this study to expand their applications to the immediate on-site monitoring of illegal additives in real samples. Concurrently, we will promote the innovative portable fluorescent nanopaper towards the commercial production stage, which is important in food processing, quality control and visual sensing.

Wenhui Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Heera Jayan: Writing – review & editing. Usman Majeed: Writing – review & editing. Xiaobo Zou: Resources, Conceptualization. Qin Hu: Supervision, Investigation, Data curation. Zhiming Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: On behalf of all the authors that contribute to this manuscript, I declare there is no interest of conflict for the work present in this paper. In addition, the paper is unpublished and has not been submitted for publication elsewhere.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32472431), National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFD2100604), Zhenjiang Science & Technology Program (GJ2024009).
